critic_raw:
This experiment has multiple fundamental design flaws, some of which interact in ways that make the results uninterpretable.

The most serious problem is the split strategy combined with the task framing. The hypothesis is about modeling patient symptom timelines — an explicitly temporal and longitudinal structure. The design then applies a stratified random split, explicitly assuming 'weak temporal autocorrelation.' But the outcome is quality-of-life score improvement at 6 and 12 months post-treatment initiation, which means the label is defined relative to a time point that varies per patient. A stratified random split of longitudinal patient records will almost certainly allow a patient's early-period records to appear in training while their later-period records (containing information predictive of their own outcome) appear in test. This is a form of within-patient temporal leakage that stratification by class does not protect against.

Second, the hypothesis compares an RNN (designed for sequential, longitudinal input) against logistic regression using 'static clinical features.' This is not a fair comparison of methods — it is a comparison of methods given fundamentally different input representations. The RNN gets the full timeline; logistic regression gets summary statistics. If the RNN wins, the experiment cannot distinguish between 'RNN is a better model' and 'sequential features contain more information than static features.' To test the hypothesis properly, you need a fair comparison: either logistic regression also gets time-series-derived features, or both models get the same feature set and only the architecture varies.

Third, the task is labeled 'regression / continuous outcome prediction' but uses MAE as the primary metric and MAPE as secondary. MAPE is undefined or unstable when quality-of-life scores approach zero, which is likely for patients with severe decline. This is not a minor metric nit — it means the evaluation will silently fail for the clinically most important patients.

Fourth, 1,500 patients over three years with an RNN with attention mechanisms is a small dataset for a sequence model. With sequences of variable length (symptom logs over months), and multi-head attention, the parameter count likely exceeds what can be reliably estimated from 1,500 training examples even with regularization. The design mentions no sample size justification and no power analysis. The RNN may simply overfit while appearing better on validation.

Fifth, the design states 'tokenization of unstructured symptom notes' but gives no details on the tokenizer, embedding source, or whether embeddings are pretrained or trained from scratch. With 1,500 patients, training embeddings from scratch on clinical text is not feasible. If a pretrained language model is used, its training corpus may contain text that encodes outcome-correlated patterns (e.g., clinical language that signals prognosis), constituting a form of external data leakage that is invisible to the evaluation design.
defender_raw:
The hypothesis is directionally sound: patient symptom timelines are inherently sequential, and RNNs with attention mechanisms are architecturally motivated to capture temporal dependencies that static logistic regression cannot model by design. The comparison is principled — the baseline logistic regression uses demographic and static clinical features, which is a realistic representation of what traditional clinical prediction systems actually deploy. This is not a strawman comparison.

The metric choice of MAE as primary with RMSE and MAPE secondary is appropriate for a continuous outcome (quality of life score improvement). MAE's linear penalty treats all prediction errors equally and is directly interpretable to clinicians as average prediction error in the same units as the outcome. The design correctly identifies this as a regression task despite the word 'logistic' appearing in the baseline description — that is a naming artifact of the model type, not the task. The evaluation protocol is internally consistent.

The stratified random split justified by "weak temporal autocorrelation" is the one genuinely debatable choice. For 1,500 patients over 3 years, if patient timelines are treated as the unit of analysis (one prediction per patient), temporal autocorrelation across patients is indeed plausible to be weak — a patient discharged in 2021 is not causally linked to one discharged in 2023 beyond shared secular trends. If the design is doing one prediction per patient using their complete timeline as input, the split is defensible. The risk is if there are policy changes or treatment protocol shifts over the 3-year window that affect outcomes systematically, random splitting will contaminate the test set with early-period data that the model shouldn't have seen.

Tokenization of unstructured symptom notes is called out explicitly and preprocessing is fit on training data only. This is a non-trivial commitment — it means the vocabulary and TF-IDF statistics (if used) are derived from training patients only, preventing the model from implicitly learning from the symptom language of test patients.

Treatment adherence as a confound is addressed directly: the design commits to monitoring adherence patterns and using adherence as a lens for interpreting prediction errors. This is honest about a major real-world confound and proposes a principled way to study it rather than ignoring it.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split of longitudinal records enables within-patient temporal leakage: early records in train, later records in test for the same patient, allowing the RNN to learn from futures it should not have access to.

The RNN receives sequential features while logistic regression receives only static features — any performance gap reflects information asymmetry, not architectural advantage, making the comparison uninterpretable.
MAPE is undefined or numerically unstable when quality-of-life improvement scores approach zero, silently corrupting results for the clinically most severe patients.
1,500 patients is insufficient to reliably train an RNN with attention mechanisms from scratch; no sample size justification or power analysis is provided.
Tokenization of symptom notes using pretrained embeddings introduces potential external data leakage if the pretrained model encodes outcome-correlated clinical language priors.
all_issues_adjudicated: The RNN receives sequential features while logistic regression receives only static features — any performance gap reflects information asymmetry, not architectural advantage, making the comparison uninterpretable.
MAPE is undefined or numerically unstable when quality-of-life improvement scores approach zero, silently corrupting results for the clinically most severe patients.
1,500 patients is insufficient to reliably train an RNN with attention mechanisms from scratch; no sample size justification or power analysis is provided.
